name: Ama-no-Nuboko
description: Ama-no-Nuboko, meaning "Heavenly Jeweled Spear," is a sacred halberd or spear featured prominently in the Japanese creation myth. It was wielded by the divine couple Izanagi and Izanami to stir the primordial ocean from the floating bridge of Heaven (Ame-no-ukihashi). As they churned the waters, salty droplets dripped from the spear, solidifying to form the first island, Onogoro-shima. This island became their residence and the foundation for the creation of the Japanese archipelago. The Ama-no-Nuboko is therefore considered a potent symbol of creation, fertility, and the divine mandate to shape the world. Its jeweled adornments are said to represent the potential for beauty and abundance within the formless void. The spear's ability to transform chaos into order highlights the power of divine intervention and the inherent connection between the heavens and the earth. It is a tangible representation of the genesis of Japan and a reminder of the deities' foundational role in shaping the land and its people. The spear is not just a weapon, but a tool for building, shaping, and bringing forth life from nothingness, embodying the creative power of the Kami.
subtitle: Heavenly Jeweled Spear of Creation
origin: Given to Izanagi and Izanami by the celestial deities
currentLocation: Unknown, possibly returned to the heavens or enshrined in a sacred location

keyMyths.title: Creation of Onogoro-shima
keyMyths.description: Izanagi and Izanami used the Ama-no-Nuboko to stir the primordial ocean, creating the first island, Onogoro-shima. This island became their home and the starting point for the creation of Japan.

keyMyths.title: Foundation of Japan
keyMyths.description: The act of stirring the ocean with the spear symbolizes the divine act of bringing order to chaos. The creation of the islands represents the establishment of the Japanese archipelago.

sources.author: Anonymous
sources.description: The oldest extant chronicle of Japan, recounting the creation myth.
sources.text: The oldest extant chronicle of Japan, recounting the creation myth.
